claims.claim: Lower cathode porosity results in higher tortuosity, which impedes Li+ ion transport in the electrolyte, thereby increasing charge transport resistance and leading to increased ohmic heat.
claims.quote: Lower porosity results in higher tortuosity of the cathode, which impedes Li+ ion transport in the electrolyte, thereby increasing the charge transport resistance. This high internal resistance increases the polarization of Li+ concentration and electrolyte potential. Consequently, according to Eq. 13, steep concentration and potential gradients ensue, leading to increased ohmic heat.

claims.claim: The maximum ohmic heating rate occurs in the vicinity of the cathode-separator interface due to higher concentration and potential gradients near that interface.
claims.quote: It is evident that the maximum ohmic heating rate occurs in the vicinity of the cathode-separator interface. This is due to the existence of higher concentration and potential gradients close to the separator-electrode interface.

claims.claim: Tortuosity significantly affects joule heating rate, while its influence on reaction and reversible heat rates is quite negligible.
claims.quote: It is important to note that tortuosity significantly affects joule heating rate, while its influence on reaction and reversible heat rates is quite negligible.

claims.claim: At high discharge rates, a low-porosity cathode (ε ≈ 0.18–0.20) causes cell self-shutdown before full discharge due to large internal resistance, which limits the maximum temperature reached even at higher C-rates.
claims.quote: The lower maximum temperature for the cell with cathode porosity of ε ∼0.18–0.20 is due to the self-shutdown of the cell. At high discharge rate, because of large internal resistance of a low-porosity cathode, the cell shuts down before the full discharge in the operating window of 3.0 - 4.2 V.

claims.claim: When the discharge current is below 7A, the maximum cell temperature within a cathode porosity range of ε ≈ 0.18–0.4 can remain outside the unsafe zone (T > 80°C) at ambient temperature of 25°C; above 7A, the cell can reach the unsafe temperature region for the same porosity range.
claims.quote: Figure 6a suggests that when the discharge current is below 7A, the maximum cell temperature rise within a cathode porosity range of ε ∼ 0.4–0.18 can remain outside the unsafe zone. However, the cell can reach the unsafe temperature region, for the same cathode porosity range, when the discharge current is higher than 7A.

claims.claim: The probability of thermal runaway in commercial lithium-ion cells type 18650 grows with increasing number of charge/discharge cycles and increasing state of charge (SOC).
claims.quote: a probability of thermal runaway in commercial lithium-ion cells of the type 18650 grows with number increase of charge/discharge cycles and increase of cells state of charge (SOC)

claims.claim: With increasing number of charge/discharge cycles, the initiation temperature of exothermic reactions of thermal runaway decreases considerably and the released energy increases.
claims.quote: with the number growth of cells charge/discharge cycles, it is observed a considerable decline of an initiation temperature of exothermic reactions of thermal runaway and increase of released energy

claims.claim: For cells with NC=45 cycles, the first group of exothermic reactions initiates at approximately 32°C and reaches a first maximum at approximately 116°C, releasing 3.29 kJ of energy.
claims.quote: the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group (Fig. 2) appear in the temperature range from 32 to 116°C... Energy of exothermic reactions of ﬁrst group... 45 [cycles]... 3.29

claims.claim: The energy released by the first group of exothermic reactions does not depend on SOC but increases with the number of charge/discharge cycles: 0 kJ at NC=0, 1.08 kJ at NC=15, 2.14 kJ at NC=30, and 3.29 kJ at NC=45.
claims.quote: Temperature ranges of the ﬁrst group of the exothermic reactions do not depend on SOC (Table I and Fig. 2). Hence, also values of the energy released by the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group do not depend on SOC. However, they depend heavily on a number of cell charge/discharge cycles (Table I).

claims.claim: The gas released during the first group of exothermic reactions consists of more than 99% hydrogen, with other gases (CO2, CO, CH4, C2H4) accounting for less than 1%, and the amount of hydrogen released does not depend on SOC but increases with the number of charge/discharge cycles.
claims.quote: The conducted measuring showed that the obtained gas from a cells consisted of hydrogen (more than 99%), while the other gases (CO2, CO, CH4 and C2H4) accounted for less than 1%... a hydrogen amount released from a cell does not depend on SOC but heavily depend on a number of charge/discharge cycles.

claims.claim: For cells with NC=45 cycles, the current interrupt device activates at approximately 75–85°C regardless of SOC, whereas for cells with NC=0, it activates at approximately 115–150°C depending on SOC, indicating greater gas release in aged cells.
claims.quote: For cells with NC = 0 (which have no exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group, Fig. 2), the current interrupt device is activated at temperature values approximately 115–150◦C depending on SOC, Fig. 3c. For cells with NC = 45 (at which the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group do take place, Fig. 2), the current interrupt device is activated approximately at the temperature 75–85◦C regardless of SOC

claims.claim: The recombination of atomic hydrogen (H + H → H2) is an exothermic reaction with heat emission of 436 kJ/mole, which is more intensive than the heat of hydrogen burning in oxygen at 285.8 kJ/mole.
claims.quote: The reaction (Eq. 3) is a powerful exothermic reaction with heat emission 436 kJ/mole (hydrogen). This heat emission is more intensive than in the reaction of hydrogen burning in oxygen and is equal to 285.8 kJ/mole (hydrogen).

claims.claim: With increasing hydrogen amount accumulated in graphite, the activation energy decreases, causing the initiation temperature of the thermal runaway exothermic reaction to decrease.
claims.quote: As with a hydrogen amount growth in graphite, its activation energy must decrease, so also an initial temperature of the exothermic reaction of the thermal runaway must decreases

claims.claim: After 45 charge/discharge cycles at 0°C, the discharge capacity of 18650 NCM/Graphite cells fell to 70% of their initial discharge capacity, which is considered the end of cell lifetime.
claims.quote: after 45 cycles of charge/discharge (at T = 0◦C), their discharge capacity fell down to 70% of their initial discharge capacity... Usually, the decrease of the cells discharge capacity down to 70% of their initial discharge capacity is considered to be the end of cells lifetime.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the temperature of thermal runaway shifts to lower values because cathode thermal stability decreases and oxygen release from the cathode increases.
claims.quote: with growth of SOC, the temperature of the thermal runaway shifts to the side of lower values. This is connected with the fact that with the SOC growth, a cathode thermal stability falls, while oxygen release from a cathode grows.

claims.claim: The SEI layer begins to decompose at 90 to 120°C, with the SEI reaction peaking at a temperature of 100°C as measured by ARC and DSC.
claims.quote: the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, which protects the anode active material from direct reaction with the electrolyte solvent, will begin to decompose at 90 to 120◦C. Using accelerated rate calorimetry (ARC) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), it has been shown that the SEI reaction peaks at a temperature of 100◦C.

claims.claim: The heat release from SEI decomposition was independent of the amount of intercalated lithium but was very sensitive to the surface area of the anode.
claims.quote: It was shown that the heat release was independent of the amount of intercalated lithium but was very sensitive to the surface area of the anode, as this increased the amount of meta-stable SEI.

claims.claim: Once the SEI layer decomposes, intercalated lithium in the anode begins to react with electrolyte solvents at temperatures greater than 120°C.
claims.quote: Once the SEI layer decomposes, it no longer protects the graphite and the intercalated lithium in the anode will begin to react with the electrolyte solvents at temperatures greater than 120◦C.

claims.claim: The positive active material can decompose and release oxygen and/or react with electrolyte solvent beginning at approximately 170°C, after the anode reactions heat the cell.
claims.quote: The first two reactions in the anode can heat the cell to temperatures where the positive active material can decompose and release oxygen and/or react with the electrolyte solvent, beginning at approximately 170◦C.

claims.claim: Electrolyte decomposition begins at temperatures greater than 200°C, and the combined heat from decomposition and combustion with released oxygen can elevate cell temperatures to over 600°C.
claims.quote: any electrolyte that remains after the reactions with the negative and positive electrodes can decompose at temperatures greater than 200◦C. The heat generated from electrolyte decomposition combined with heat released during combustion with released oxygen can elevate cell temperatures to over 600◦C.
